justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-8 actually covers 8 rules, exceeding the minimum). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-5, record visibility BR-6, record lock irreversibility BR-8) correctly marked invariant: true. Operational rules (cancellation window BR-3, admin override BR-4, 48h threshold BR-7) appropriately marked invariant: false with nuanced justification. Role-based cancellation correctly captured as separate rules (BR-3 patient, BR-4 admin). 14 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum of 4, covering all required scenarios: booking licensed physician (AC-1/AC-2), double-booking (AC-3/AC-4), late cancellation (AC-5/AC-6/AC-7), unauthorized record access (AC-8/AC-9/AC-10). Confidence at 72 is within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation mechanism and treating physician scope.

observations.text: Exceptional invariant classification — BR-7 (48h lock) marked invariant: false with the nuanced reasoning that the concept of locking is invariant but the threshold is soft, while BR-8 (lock irreversibility) correctly separated as invariant: true
14 acceptance criteria with precise boundary conditions (AC-4 adjacent slots, AC-14 exact 48h boundary) far exceeds the 4 minimum
Rich edge cases covering concurrency, license revocation post-booking, cancelled appointment access rights, and boundary precision — all operationally significant
Escalations are well-structured with options and recommendations, demonstrating genuine domain reasoning rather than rote rule extraction
Correctly identified both bounded contexts (appointment scheduling and medical records) in the domain analysis

Confidence level of 72 with specific high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions about license verification being binary and treating physician scope — exactly the kind of assumption-noting the rubric requires
